latest.title: Second tranche of share buyback programme
latest.content:
1 May 2026 07:00
RNS Number : 6884C
McBride PLC
01 May 2026
McBride plc
Second tranche of share buyback programme
McBride plc (the "
Company
"), the leading European manufacturer and supplier of private label and contract manufactured products for the domestic household and professional cleaning/hygiene markets, today announces the commencement of the second tranche of its share buyback programme to purchase ordinary shares of 10 pence each in the Company ("
Ordinary Shares
"), as launched on 1 December 2025 (the "
Programme
").
Purchases under the first tranche of the Programme commenced on 1 December 2025 and continued until 30 April 2026. Under the first tranche, between 1 December 2025 and 30 April 2026, the Company repurchased 3,236,565 Ordinary Shares for an aggregate consideration of approximately £4.5 million.
The Company announces that the second tranche of the Programme of up to £15.5 million (being the balance of the £20 million maximum amount of the Programme less the aggregate consideration paid under the first tranche of the Programme) will commence today (the "
Second Tranche
"). The Second Tranche is structured in two phases. In respect of the first phase, the Company has entered into a non-discretionary agreement with Investec Bank plc ("
Investec
") to act as riskless principal in relation to the purchase of Ordinary Shares, and the subsequent sale of such Ordinary Shares to the Company. The second phase will be undertaken by Peel Hunt LLP ("
Peel Hunt
", together with Investec, the "
Brokers
"). The Company has entered into a non-discretionary agreement with Peel Hunt to act as riskless principal in relation to the purchase of Ordinary Shares, and the subsequent sale of such Ordinary Shares to the Company.
Purchases of Ordinary Shares under the Second Tranche are expected to commence on 1 May 2026 and to be completed by no later than 30 September 2026. The Company intends to announce any purchases under the Programme on a weekly basis from the commencement of the Second Tranche of the Programme.
The Brokers will each make trading decisions in relation to the Programme independently of the Company. Any purchase of Ordinary Shares by the Brokers contemplated by this announcement will be carried out on the London Stock Exchange. Any purchases of Ordinary Shares by the Company under the Programme will be carried out on the London Stock Exchange.
The purpose of the Programme is to reduce the share capital of the Company by returning surplus capital to shareholders. Ordinary Shares purchased under the Second Tranche will be cancelled. These arrangements are in accordance with the Company's general authority to repurchase up to 17,401,528 Ordinary Shares granted at its 2025 Annual General Meeting. The Second Tranche will also be conducted within certain pre-set parameters, in accordance with Chapter 9 of the Listing Rules and within the parameters prescribed by the Market Abuse Regulation 596/2014/EU and the Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2016/1052 (both as incorporated into UK domestic law by the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018).
From time to time, the Board will assess the progress of this Programme in light of the financial situation of the Company and its capital allocation needs.
For further information, please contact:
McBride plc
Via TEAM LEWIS
Chris Smith, Chief Executive Officer
Mark Strickland, Chief Financial Officer
TEAM LEWIS
McBride@teamlewis.com
Hannah Scott
Galyna Kulachek
+44 (0)20 7802 2634
+44 (0)20 7802 2617

McBride PLC
01 May 2026

Transaction in Own Shares
McBride plc (the "Company") announces today it has purchased the following number of its ordinary shares of 10 pence each ("Ordinary Shares") through Peel Hunt LLP (the "Broker").
Ordinary Shares
Date of purchase

59,332

158.20

159.00
Volume weighted average price per day (pence):
158.25
The Company will purchase the above Ordinary Shares through the Broker on the London Stock Exchange (the "Purchases") and will subsequently cancel them.
Such Purchases form part of the Company's existing buyback programme and were effected pursuant to the non-discretionary agreement entered into with the Broker, as announced on 1st December 2025.
Following the Purchases and cancellation of these Ordinary Shares, the Company will have 175,281,297 Ordinary Shares in issue (excluding treasury shares), hold 42,041 Ordinary Shares in treasury, and have 594,610,458 non-cumulative redeemable preference shares of 0.1 pence each in the Company ("B Shares") in issue. B Shares carry no rights to attend, speak or vote at Company meetings, except on a resolution relating to the winding up of the Company.
Once the Ordinary Shares are cancelled, the total number of voting rights in the Company will be 175,281,297. This figure may be used by shareholders of the Company as the denominator for the calculations by which they can determine if they are required to notify their interest, or a change to their interest, in the Company pursuant to the Disclosure and Transparency Rules (the "DTRs").
The Company confirms that, in accordance with DTR 5.5.1R, following the Purchases the percentage of voting rights attributable to the Ordinary Shares held in treasury is 0.02 per cent.
The tables below contain detailed information about the Purchases.

company_30s.what_they_do: McBride plc makes and sells household and professional cleaning products under private‑label contracts. Think of the familiar “M&J” detergent, dish soap, surface cleaners and other hygiene items that you see in supermarkets and supermarkets’ own brands.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The company sits in a huge, low‑margin market that is driven by consumer staples. Because it supplies private‑label brands, it enjoys a steady stream of long‑term contracts and a low customer‑switching rate. Its European footprint gives it access to a large, stable revenue base that is less sensitive to economic cycles than high‑growth tech.
company_30s.current_state: McBride is in a consolidation phase. It has recently finished a multi‑stage share‑buyback, announced a €34.5m purchase of Eurotab, and is tightening costs. The business is still profitable but is tightening margins as raw‑material prices rise. The share is trading around £1.6, well below the 52‑week high of roughly £3.5, indicating a deep slide that has not yet been fully priced in.

rns_narrative: The company is executing a systematic buy‑back program that signals confidence in its cash flow. That same program is being used to fund the acquisition of Eurotab, a niche player in solid‑format cleaning solutions, which will broaden McBride’s product line.

Management is tightening costs, announcing a 5‑year strategic plan that includes a focus on private‑label profitability and a modest increase in revenue forecasts. The interim results show stable earnings, but the guidance is cautious, reflecting higher input costs.

The board is also restructuring the directorate, which usually signals a shift in strategic focus. All of these moves point to a company that is stabilising after a period of volatility and is now looking to consolidate its market position.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Quiet optimism – traders are largely silent, but those who are speaking see the buy‑back and acquisition as signs of a potential rebound.
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: Bullish voices focus on the buy‑back as a catalyst that will lift the share price and on the Eurotab deal as a way to add higher‑margin products.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Bears worry that cost inflation could erode margins faster than the company expects, and that the acquisition might not integrate cleanly, leaving the share price stuck in a low‑range.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: Sentiment has shifted from near‑zero chatter to a small cluster of bullish posts after the buy‑back announcement, but overall engagement remains minimal – a classic contrarian sign.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends is flat, meaning the public is not looking for McBride. A quiet backdrop is a perfect set‑up for a surprise bounce when the next catalyst hits.

near_term_catalysts: The next tranche of the share‑buyback, scheduled for early June, could act as a price floor if the market treats the buy‑back as a sign of confidence.
An earnings report in July will likely show whether the cost‑control measures are working; a better‑than‑expected margin would trigger a rally.
If the Eurotab integration releases a new product line in the summer, the increased product mix could lift demand and push the share higher.
A spike in trading volume above 500,000 shares on any day would be a clear sign that institutional capital is moving back in.
expect_next.watch_for: Look for a break above the 12‑week simple moving average, a volume spike that exceeds the 20‑day average by 30%, and a move that takes the price to the 50‑day low (around £1.2) – that would confirm a mean‑reversion play.
expect_next.timing_regime: The stock is still in the early stages of a deep crash cycle – the price has already fallen over 70% from the all‑time high. The APEX score is high, indicating a tight support zone. A bounce would likely start once the buy‑back completes and the market starts to value the company’s fundamentals again.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: If the share is down 80% from its peak, a recovery to the mid‑point (roughly 50% of the high) would give a 2.5× return. The historical data for deep crashes (GREEN/ORANGE) shows average returns of 39–113%, and the recent buy‑back suggests the market will start to re‑price the stock. The mean‑reversion thesis is strong because the company has solid cash flow and a stable contract base.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: The biggest risk is that the cost‑inflation cycle could continue, squeezing margins further. If the acquisition fails to deliver expected synergies, the share could stay stuck near the 12‑week low. A worst‑case scenario is a drop to £0.80, which would be a 50% loss from the current price.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Potential upside: 2.5× (to £2.40 from £1.00). Potential downside: 50% (to £0.80). Risk‑reward ratio roughly 5:1, heavily skewed in favour of the upside.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter after a volume spike that pushes the price above £1.20. Use a position size that limits exposure to 1–2% of the portfolio. Place a stop 20% below the entry (around £0.96). Trail the stop upward once the price passes the 12‑week moving average.
playbook.if_watching: Wait for the next buy‑back tranche announcement and for a clear volume surge. The trigger price is £1.20 with volume > 500k shares. If the price falls below £1.00, consider tightening the stop or re‑evaluating.
playbook.if_passing: Not applicable – there are no imminent delisting or bankruptcy risks.

ai_insights.bottom_line: McBride is a deep‑crashed, cost‑controlled household cleaning business that just finished a buy‑back and acquisition; if the next tranche triggers a volume spike, the stock could rebound to mid‑point levels, offering a 2.5× upside against a 50% downside.
